source.title: Learning Invariant Object and Spatial View Representations in the Brain Using Slow Unsupervised Learning

confidence_rationale: Rolls' VisNet model demonstrates computationally that separate invariant object and spatial representations can be learned through the same slow-learning principle but in different cortical streams. Directly supports the architectural separation SD-015 proposes. Confidence moderated by VisNet being a specific computational hypothesis rather than established neural architecture.
failure_signatures: VisNet relies on temporal continuity (slow feature analysis) for invariance learning -- REE's ResourceEncoder uses supervised or BCE training, a fundamentally different learning signal.
Rolls' spatial view cells are allocentric and hippocampal -- REE's z_world is egocentric grid observation, a different coordinate frame.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The brain learns position-invariant object representations in the ventral visual stream (IT cortex) and allocentric spatial view representations in the parietal/posterior cingulate cortex using the same slow unsupervised learning principle, but these two types of representation are learned in architecturally separate streams that feed the hippocampus for memory and navigation.
mapping.ree_translation: SD-015 proposes an analogous architectural separation: z_resource (object-type features, invariant to location) is learned by a dedicated ResourceEncoder separate from z_world (full scene encoding including spatial layout). Both feed into the E3 trajectory scoring system, paralleling how ventral object and dorsal spatial representations both converge on hippocampus.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Rolls' model concerns biological learning of invariant representations from raw visual input via temporal continuity. REE's ResourceEncoder is a 2-layer MLP trained on structured grid observations. The architectural principle (separate streams for what vs where) transfers; the learning mechanism does not.
mapping.source_context: Computational neuroscience model (VisNet) of primate visual cortex, validated against IT cortex and parietal cortex neurophysiology

confidence_components.notes: Rolls is a major figure in this area and VisNet has substantial validation. Mapping fidelity good because the paper explicitly addresses the what/where separation for navigation. Transfer risk moderate -- the architectural principle is strong but the learning mechanism differs.
